factual-accuracy.requirement: States TWFE coefficient is variance-weighted average of all 2×2 DiD contrasts including forbidden comparisons using already-treated as controls
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that TWFE can be decomposed into a weighted average of all possible 2×2 DiD contrasts and explicitly notes it includes comparisons that use already-treated units as controls ("forbidden comparisons"). This matches the criterion’s description of variance/weighting and inclusion of already-treated-as-controls contrasts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains TWFE produces negative or non-convex weights when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or time
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that under heterogeneous treatment effects TWFE can assign “negative and non-convex weights,” including by using already-treated units as controls, which can bias estimates and even reverse signs.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes TWFE can produce biased estimates or wrong-sign results even when all true effects have same sign
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that TWFE can place negative weights under heterogeneous effects and that this can yield misleading or counterintuitive sign reversals, even when underlying effects share the same sign.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Callaway-Sant'Anna estimates group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) for each cohort g in each period t
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Callaway and Sant’Anna’s framework is centered around “group-time average treatment effects (ATT(g,t))” defined as the average effect for group g first treated at time g evaluated at time t, which implies estimating ATT(g,t) for each cohort and period.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains CS uses only clean controls (never-treated or not-yet-treated) for each ATT(g,t) estimate then aggregates flexibly
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Callaway and Sant’Anna estimate ATT(g,t) using “never-treated or not-yet-treated” (clean) comparison groups and then describes “flexible methods aggregate heterogeneous ATT(g,t)s using weighting schemes,” which matches the criterion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes CS allows flexible aggregation to overall ATT, event-study paths, cohort-specific effects, or calendar-time averages
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Callaway and Sant’Anna’s framework as estimating ATT(g,t) and then using flexible aggregation schemes to summarize effects, including dynamic/event-time paths and cohort-specific effects, as well as calendar-time-related summaries.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies CS requires cohort-specific parallel trends relative to chosen control set
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Callaway & Sant’Anna’s use of clean controls and conditional parallel trends, but it does not state that CS requires cohort-specific parallel trends relative to the chosen control set. It never explicitly frames the identifying assumption as cohort-by-cohort (group-specific) parallel trends tied to the selected control group.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CS allows explicit anticipation windows where pre-specified periods can be dropped or excluded from control
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Callaway and Sant’Anna’s handling of anticipation only in general terms (e.g., “limited anticipation” and sensitivity analyses) and does not state that CS allows explicit anticipation windows where pre-specified periods can be dropped/excluded from the control group.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names R package 'did' or Stata command 'csdid' for Callaway-Sant'Anna implementation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly names the R package “did” as the software implementation for Callaway and Sant’Anna’s approach.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Sun-Abraham interacts cohort indicators with relative event-time dummies using cohort-share weights
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Sun and Abraham as using “interaction-weighted” estimators and event-time effects, but it does not state that cohort indicators are interacted with relative event-time dummies using cohort-share weights. It also does not mention cohort-share weighting explicitly or explain that specific weighting scheme.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains SA uses clean controls (never-treated or last-treated) and non-negative weights to avoid contamination from already-treated
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Sun and Abraham use only never-treated or last-treated groups as controls and that this avoids negative weighting and contamination from already-treated units. It also describes Callaway and Sant’Anna as using clean controls (never-treated or not-yet-treated) to avoid forbidden comparisons.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes SA produces explicit relative-time event-study framework with interpretable coefficients at each event time
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Sun and Abraham’s event-study approach as estimating event-time specific effects (“relative event times”) with interpretable coefficients and discusses pointwise confidence intervals for each event time. This constitutes an explicit relative-time event-study framework with interpretable coefficients at each event time.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States SA has intuitive OLS-based implementation or is common default for plotting dynamics
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not mention “SA” at all, nor does it state that SA has an intuitive OLS-based implementation or that it is a common default for plotting dynamics. It discusses Sun & Abraham’s interaction-weighted event-study estimator and related software, but never characterizes it as an intuitive OLS default for dynamic plots.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names 'eventstudyinteract', 'sunab' in fixest, or similar package for Sun-Abraham
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly names the Stata package ‘eventstudyinteract’ as the software implementation for Sun and Abraham’s estimator.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains BJS estimates untreated outcome model using only untreated observations then imputes counterfactual Y(0) for treated units
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Borusyak et al.’s method as imputing untreated potential outcomes for treated units, but it does not state that the untreated outcome model is estimated using only untreated observations before imputing Y(0) for treated units. It lacks the specific two-step detail (fit on untreated-only, then impute counterfactuals for treated).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States BJS obtains individual treatment effects τit by subtracting imputed Y(0) from observed outcomes for treated observations
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Borusyak et al.’s imputation approach in general terms but never states that BJS obtains individual treatment effects τ_it by subtracting an imputed Y(0) from observed outcomes for treated observations. It does not mention τ_it or this specific subtraction-based construction for treated units.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes BJS requires untreated potential outcomes follow additive unit and time fixed effects (stronger functional form than CS)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess’ imputation approach but does not note that BJS requires untreated potential outcomes to follow an additive unit and time fixed-effects structure. It also does not explicitly contrast this stronger functional-form requirement with Callaway–Sant’Anna’s approach.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States BJS achieves efficiency gains by pooling all untreated observations including pre-treatment periods
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess’ imputation approach but does not state that BJS achieves efficiency gains by pooling all untreated observations including pre-treatment periods. It never mentions pooling all untreated observations or explicitly ties that pooling to efficiency gains. This specific mechanism is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains did2s estimates unit and time effects on untreated observations in stage 1 then regresses residualized outcomes on treatment in stage 2
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Borusyak et al.’s imputation approach as imputing untreated outcomes and mentions a “single-step OLS regression,” but it does not explain the specific did2s two-stage procedure of estimating unit and time effects using only untreated observations in stage 1 and then regressing residualized outcomes on treatment in stage 2.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes did2s yields estimates equivalent to particular imputation scheme via two standard OLS steps
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Borusyak et al.’s imputation approach in general terms but does not note that did2s yields estimates equivalent to a particular imputation scheme implemented via two standard OLS steps. It also does not explain the two-step OLS procedure or the equivalence mapping between did2s and imputation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States all three methods (CS, SA, BJS) allow unrestricted treatment effect heterogeneity across cohorts and event time
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly says Callaway–Sant’Anna allows “full treatment effect heterogeneity across groups and over time,” and it states Sun–Abraham and Borusyak–Jaravel–Spiess permit “heterogeneous and dynamic treatment effects.” It does not clearly state that all three allow unrestricted heterogeneity specifically across both cohorts and event time for each method (especially BJS in event-time terms).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Roth (2022) identifies low power where standard pre-trend tests fail to detect violations large enough to materially bias treatment effect estimates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes Roth (2022) argues that null/insignificant pre-trend tests are insufficient and can miss underlying violations, but it does not specifically state that Roth identifies low power where standard pre-trend tests fail to detect violations large enough to materially bias treatment effect estimates. The required “low power” and “material bias” framing is not explicitly included.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains Roth notes inference conditional on passing pre-trend tests creates selection bias and nominal confidence intervals may undercover
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Roth’s critique of pre-trend testing but does not explain Roth’s specific inference point that conditioning on passing pre-trend tests induces selection bias and can cause nominal confidence intervals to undercover. It mentions that null pre-trends are insufficient and suggests sensitivity/bounding, but omits the selection-on-significance mechanism and undercoverage implication.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes SA cleans pre-period coefficients of cross-cohort contamination making nonzero leads more diagnostic of genuine violations
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not note that Sun and Abraham “cleans” pre-period coefficients of cross-cohort contamination, nor does it explain that this makes nonzero lead estimates more diagnostic of genuine parallel-trends violations. It only says SA provides pointwise CIs for pre-treatment event times and cautions about interpretation, missing the specific contamination-cleaning point.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States BJS offers pre-trend test estimated on untreated observations only to reduce inference-after-pretest distortions
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Roth (2022) and mentions pre-trend testing generally, but it never states that BJS offers a pre-trend test estimated on untreated observations only or that this is to reduce inference-after-pretest distortions. There is no mention of BJS’s specific pre-trend testing procedure or the untreated-only estimation feature.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Discusses Rambachan-Roth honest DiD confidence sets or bounds valid under specified classes of parallel trends violations
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly references “Rambachan and Roth’s methods” and discusses using bounding/sensitivity analyses to assess robustness to parallel trends violations, which corresponds to honest DiD confidence sets or bounds under specified classes of violations.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Advises displaying pre-trend estimates but avoiding conditioning main inference on pass/fail tests
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Roth (2022) by noting that null pre-trends are insufficient and recommends moving beyond simple pre-trend tests toward “transparent diagnostic visualizations” and sensitivity/bounding approaches, implying pre-trends should be shown but not used as a pass/fail gate for inference.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2020-2021 period showed awareness of Goodman-Bacon critique with TWFE kept but scrutinized
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly notes that TWFE remained common in 2020–2024 but that many papers acknowledged its limitations, used Goodman-Bacon decompositions, and adopted alternative estimators—indicating awareness of the critique while keeping TWFE under scrutiny.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CS is widely used in labor economics applications for transparency and flexible aggregation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions software like Stata’s `csdid` but does not state that CS is widely used in labor economics applications, nor does it attribute that usage to transparency and flexible aggregation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies modal practice as triangulation using SA for dynamic figures and CS or BJS for level effects
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not identify a modal applied practice as triangulating across Sun-Abraham for dynamic/event-study effects and Callaway–Sant’Anna or Borusyak–Jaravel–Spiess for level/overall effects. It discusses each method and claims C&S dominance, but it never frames the field’s modal practice as this specific triangulation pattern.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Goodman-Bacon decomposition as diagnostic tool to assess severity of forbidden comparisons
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly mentions using the “Goodman-Bacon Decomposition” as a diagnostic to detect contamination/negative weighting and discusses “forbidden comparisons,” framing the decomposition as a tool to assess these issues.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides if-then guidance for choosing estimators based on 3+ design features (never-treated, timing density, goal)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response compares Callaway–Sant’Anna, Sun–Abraham, and Borusyak et al., but it does not give explicit if-then decision rules for choosing among estimators. It also does not clearly condition choices on 3+ design features like presence of never-treated units, density of treatment timing, and the analyst’s goal (e.g., event-study dynamics vs overall ATT).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Ranks assumption strength across estimators (e.g., notes CS nonparametric PT is weaker than BJS additive FE functional form)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response compares assumptions across the main estimators, describing TWFE as requiring homogeneity, CS as allowing heterogeneous ATT(g,t) under conditional parallel trends, SA as interaction-weighted event-study under generalized parallel trends, and BJS as imputation-based with outcome-model assumptions. This effectively ranks/contrasts assumption strength and notes differing functional-form/modeling commitments across approaches.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Presents 2+ competing views on estimator dominance or acknowledges no consensus and uncertainty about definitive bibliometric evidence
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response presents competing views by stating Callaway–Sant’Anna and Sun–Abraham are most frequent while Borusyak et al. is growing but less adopted, and it also notes that TWFE remains common. It further hedges with uncertainty language like “evidence suggests” and “varies by field and journal,” acknowledging lack of definitive consensus/bibliometric proof.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Traces practice evolution through 3+ time periods (2020-2021, 2022-2023, 2023-2024) noting specific changes in each phase
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not trace applied DiD practice evolution across 3+ distinct time periods (2020-2021, 2022-2023, 2023-2024) with specific changes in each phase. It discusses methods and gives a general 2020–2024 adoption narrative, but it never breaks adoption or practices into those three periods. The required period-by-period evolution is missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains transparency vs efficiency trade-off (e.g., CS disaggregated ATT(g,t) display vs BJS pooling untreated observations)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses Callaway & Sant’Anna’s disaggregated ATT(g,t) framework and contrasts it with Borusyak et al.’s imputation approach emphasizing computational efficiency, which reflects a transparency-versus-efficiency trade-off between showing granular effects and pooling/model-based estimation.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects specific estimator features (SA contamination cleaning, BJS untreated-only tests) to addressing Roth's low-power or conditioning bias
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses avoiding “forbidden comparisons” and mentions clean control groups and Roth’s pre-trend critique, but it does not connect specific estimator features like SA contamination cleaning or BJS untreated-only tests to addressing Roth’s low-power or conditioning-bias concerns. Those concrete feature-to-Roth linkages are absent.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Assigns specific validation roles to multiple estimators (SA for dynamics, CS for transparency, BJS for efficiency) in reporting workflow
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses Callaway–Sant’Anna, Sun–Abraham, and Borusyak–Jaravel–Spiess methods, but it never assigns validation roles to multiple estimators (SA for dynamics, CS for transparency, BJS for efficiency) within a reporting workflow. No such role-based workflow mapping appears anywhere in the text.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends conditioning main inference on passing pre-trend tests without addressing low-power or conditioning bias concerns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses Roth (2022) by warning that null pre-trends are insufficient and recommends sensitivity analyses and bounding approaches rather than conditioning inference on passing pre-trend tests. It does not advise proceeding only if pre-trend tests pass, nor does it ignore low-power/conditioning-bias concerns.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States TWFE produces unbiased or reliable estimates when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or time
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response characterizes TWFE as biased under heterogeneous treatment effects (negative weights, forbidden comparisons) and says it implicitly requires homogeneous effects, rather than claiming TWFE is unbiased or reliable under heterogeneity.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses exact econometric terminology (ATT, parallel trends, TWFE, cohort-specific effects) without simplifying for lay audiences
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses exact econometric terminology such as TWFE, ATT(g,t), parallel trends, heterogeneous treatment effects, and cohort/group-time effects, and it does so in a technical register rather than simplifying for lay audiences.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses systematic structure (numbered sections, headers, bullet points) for multi-method comparison
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized with clear headers and subheaders (e.g., sections for each method and assumption category) and uses bullet points throughout to structure the multi-method comparison.

presentation-quality.requirement: Acknowledges competing methodological views or uncertainty without asserting false consensus
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses hedging language (e.g., “evidence suggests,” “appear most frequently,” “increasingly,” “not universal,” “varies by field and journal”) and notes ongoing evolution, rather than claiming a settled consensus about dominance or adoption.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains basic econometric concepts (what DiD is, what fixed effects are) that an applied economist would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes basic explanations of DiD and fixed-effects/TWFE concepts (e.g., describing DiD as a cornerstone, defining TWFE and staggered adoption, and explaining weighted averages of 2×2 DiDs), which are introductory for an applied economist.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same methodological points or recommendations multiple times without adding new technical detail
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeats several high-level recommendations multiple times (e.g., that newer estimators avoid “forbidden comparisons/negative weights,” that they “relax/generalize parallel trends,” and that researchers should use “sensitivity analyses/robustness checks”) across sections like the assumptions comparison, Roth discussion, and conclusion without adding much new technical detail each time.

citation-quality.requirement: References Goodman-Bacon (2021) on TWFE decomposition in staggered adoption settings
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Goodman-Bacon (2021) and describes his TWFE decomposition as a weighted average of 2×2 DiD comparisons in staggered adoption settings, including discussion of negative weights and forbidden comparisons.

citation-quality.requirement: References Callaway and Sant'Anna methodology for group-time ATT estimation
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly discusses “Callaway and Sant’Anna’s Two-Stage Aggregation Approach” and describes their group-time ATT(g,t) framework for estimating heterogeneous effects under staggered adoption.

citation-quality.requirement: References Sun and Abraham interaction-weighted estimator
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly discusses “Sun and Abraham’s Interaction-Weighted Estimators” and repeatedly refers to their interaction-weighted event-study approach.

citation-quality.requirement: References Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (BJS) imputation-based estimator

citation-quality.requirement: References Roth (2022) on low power and conditioning bias in pre-trend testing
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly discusses Roth (2022) in the section on pre-trend testing, describing his concerns about low power and how pre-trend tests can be misleading/insufficient for validating parallel trends.

citation-quality.requirement: References Rambachan-Roth honest DiD sensitivity analysis methodology
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Rambachan and Roth’s sensitivity analysis methodology, noting “Rambachan and Roth’s methods” and listing “Rambachan and Roth (2022) Bounded Deviation Sensitivity Analyses” in the sources.

citation-quality.requirement: Discusses adoption patterns in econometrics journals (AER, QJE, JPE) for 2020-2024 period with field-specific examples
citation-quality.explanation: The response mentions AER, QJE, and JPE and makes general claims about increasing adoption, but it does not actually discuss 2020–2024 adoption patterns with concrete, field-specific examples from labor or health papers in those journals.
